coredata.dc:title: “Quantum dots: Perspectives in next-generation chemical gas sensors” ‒ A review
coredata.dc:creator: Galstyan, Vardan
coredata.dc:description: Advances in gas sensing devices are urgently needed to reduce air pollution and control human health. In this regard, the progress in the development of low-dimensional semiconductor structures may revolutionize the concept of conventional gas sensors. The confinement of charge carriers in one or more spatial dimensions leads to the unique electrical and optical properties of semiconductor materials. Quantum dots, where the electron-hole pairs are confined in all three dimensions, offer new insights into the properties of materials. The research on quantum dot chemical sensors has become one of the rapidly developing fields in contemporary sensing technologies. The structures comprising quantum dots have shown promising sensing performance indicating that they are emerging as a new class of materials for application in chemiresistive devices. However, the QD-based structures are only beginning to be integrated into the monitoring systems. The experimental findings suggest that intensive studies need to be performed for deeply understanding the influence of synthesis procedures and additive materials on the sensing performance of quantum dots. Moreover, the response and selectivity of the materials should be analyzed considering the band gap changes in quantum dots as the size is varied. This paper provides an overview of the progress in the research of semiconductor quantum dots for application in chemical gas sensors. Advances in the fabrication and functionalities of metal oxide, chalcogenide and carbon quantum dots are highlighted. The effect of precursor materials and preparation methods on the structural features, chemical nature, size reduction and electronic properties of quantum dots are considered to examine their sensing performance. Afterward, a brief summary and outlook for the field are provided, along with the achievements and issues that are important for future studies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This paper is dedicated to the memory of Dr. Valeri M. Arakelyan from Yerevan State University who passed away recently. He was a brilliant scientist, a wonderful person and a family man. Dr. V. M. Arakelyan was working on the development of photoelectrochemical converters and gas sensing systems achieving appreciable results in the aforementioned fields. I also would like to thank the healthcare workers of Italy fighting COVID-19 to ensure our health and achieve a safe work environment during this challenging time.

head.ce:title: “Quantum dots: Perspectives in next-generation chemical gas sensors” ‒ A review

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sensor Lab, Department of Information Engineering, University of Brescia, Via Valotti 9, 25133 Brescia, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Advances in gas sensing devices are urgently needed to reduce air pollution and control human health. In this regard, the progress in the development of low-dimensional semiconductor structures may revolutionize the concept of conventional gas sensors. The confinement of charge carriers in one or more spatial dimensions leads to the unique electrical and optical properties of semiconductor materials. Quantum dots, where the electron-hole pairs are confined in all three dimensions, offer new insights into the properties of materials. The research on quantum dot chemical sensors has become one of the rapidly developing fields in contemporary sensing technologies. The structures comprising quantum dots have shown promising sensing performance indicating that they are emerging as a new class of materials for application in chemiresistive devices. However, the QD-based structures are only beginning to be integrated into the monitoring systems. The experimental findings suggest that intensive studies need to be performed for deeply understanding the influence of synthesis procedures and additive materials on the sensing performance of quantum dots. Moreover, the response and selectivity of the materials should be analyzed considering the band gap changes in quantum dots as the size is varied. This paper provides an overview of the progress in the research of semiconductor quantum dots for application in chemical gas sensors. Advances in the fabrication and functionalities of metal oxide, chalcogenide and carbon quantum dots are highlighted. The effect of precursor materials and preparation methods on the structural features, chemical nature, size reduction and electronic properties of quantum dots are considered to examine their sensing performance. Afterward, a brief summary and outlook for the field are provided, along with the achievements and issues that are important for future studies.

ce:section.ce:para:
Nowadays, the application of low-dimensional materials in functional devices is of great interest. In these structures, the properties of matter change dramatically and microelectronics passes into the field of nanoelectronics, where the operation of devices is based on quantum effects [1,2]. Low-dimensional materials with their quantum-mechanical properties can directly affect the features of functional devices [2–5]. The performance of these materials depends on the quantum confinement (QC) of mobile charge carriers [6]. The QC of carriers can be evaluated based on their de Broglie wavelength (
λ
d e
B
) [7]. According to the de Broglie theory, a moving charge carrier with a mass of
m
along a single direction in the crystal may act as a wave given by equation Eq. (1) [7,8]:
(1)
λ
d e
B
=
h
m
K B
T l
where
h
is the Planck constant,
K B
is the Boltzmann constant and
T l
is the temperature of the crystal lattice. Low-dimensional materials are classified considering the number of directions in which the size of the structure is comparable to the de Broglie wavelength of its free electron [8]. Zero-dimensional (0D) semiconductor nanoparticles, where the motion of charge carriers is confined in all three directions, are called quantum dots (QDs) [9]. In this case, the energy difference between energy bands is changed depending on the size of the particle. Thus, it is possible to tune the band gap of QDs by changing their size (Fig. 1 [9–11]). The latter is one of the main advantages of QDs as their electrical and optical properties can be engineered by precise control of the dot size [9,11–13]. QDs are composed of a relatively small number of atoms. The electrons in these atomic clusters are totally confined and the energy spectrum is totally discrete [14,15]. The discrete spectrum of energy states in such clusters is similar to the energy spectrum of individual atoms. Due to this similarity, the QDs are called “artificial” atoms [8]. The unique properties of QDs make it possible to overcome the limitations that arise from using bulk materials [16]. QDs have exhibited outstanding potential for applications in functional devices [17–20]. In the meantime, the extremely small dimensions and the widely tunable band gap of QDs attracted great interest by the scientific community of gas sensors [21,22]. The variation of the aforementioned parameters can affect the adsorption and desperation processes of gaseous compounds on the semiconductor materials playing a key role in the detection of different gases in the environment. Moreover, the demand for real-time monitoring of air pollution levels and health status is continuously growing due to the worldwide environmental and healthcare issues. Consequently, the requirements for modern sensing systems have been increased [23–28].

Different semiconductor QD structures have been extensively investigated to replace the conventional bulk materials in conductometric sensors [21,29–31]. The synthesis procedure, composition and structure of QDs have a crucial effect on their sensing performance. QD-containing sensors offer several advantages over conventional thick-film devices in terms of improved charge transport and enhanced surface area, opening new pathways for the development of next-generation portable sensing systems [32,33].

Considering the aforementioned points, it is necessary to understand the current scenario of scientific research and publications on the chemiresistive sensors comprising QDs. Herein, the potential and perspectives of semiconductor QDs for applications in chemical gas sensors have been comprehensively discussed including their synergetic effect in composite structures.

The growing interest in QDs for different technological applications has stimulated the development of their synthesis methods. The semiconductor QDs have been prepared by epitaxial growth and colloidal synthesis techniques [34,35]. Epitaxial method provides an ordered growth of QDs, where the temperature, structure and crystallographic orientation of the substrate should be considered in detail [36,37]. The synthesis procedures are performed through the liquid-phase epitaxy (LPE), metal-organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) and molecular beam epitaxy (MBE) [38–40]. The growth control of QDs during the epitaxy process considering the principles of thermodynamics and kinetics leads to reproducible structures. LPE method was applied for the preparation of self-assembled lead sulfide (PbS) QDs [38]. The difference in lattice structures between the PbSe and the substrate material affected the growth of QDs more than the difference in their lattice constants. However, the lattice mismatch plays a significant effect on the shape of QDs during the heteroepitaxy procedure [41], while the chemical modification in the composition of the substrate surface may increase the density of QDs prepared by LPE [42].

The MOCVD method can be used for the fabrication of ZnO QDs [39]. The obtained experimental results indicated that the substrate temperature affected the adsorption and desorption of atoms during the nucleation process. The increase in the substrate temperature worsened the homogeneity of ZnO QDs and decreased their density (Fig. 2 ). However, the migration length and the adsorption of atoms can be enhanced at elevated temperatures promoting the nucleation of QDs during the MOCVD procedure [43]. Therefore, the diameter and density of QDs should be controlled by adjusting the substrate temperature.

MBE method can be used for the preparation of a wide range of semiconductor QDs, where the crystallinity, density and shape of structures can be controlled depending on the gas source, the molecular beams of precursor materials and the buffer layer [40,44–48]. The substrate preparation procedure is very important for MBE growth technique because the impurities in the substrate may act as nucleation sites [49]. Furthermore, MBE is an efficient method for the fabrication of doped and complex structures, which enables the reduction of energy of QD confined levels [34].

Unlike the epitaxial growth processes, the colloidal synthesis methods do not require expensive facilities and technological procedures [50]. Since the first reports on the preparation of colloidal QDs [51,52], this chemical approach has been intensively developed to fabricate different semiconductor QDs [1,22,53]. Colloidal synthesis is a liquid-phase condensation, wherein the chemical reactions supersaturate the precursors and cause nucleation of QDs. Therefore, the precursor solutions and the conditions of chemical reactions are the key factors for the preparation of QDs [54].

Colloidal SnO2 QDs were synthesized using tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O), oleic acid (OA) and alcohols [55]. The chemical reaction procedure was carried out in Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C. The morphological analysis of samples showed that the dimensions of QDs were increased with an increase in reaction time. The QDs were transformed into nanowires when the reaction lasted more than 4 h. Nevertheless, OA and oleylamine (OLA) ligands interact with the surface of SnO2 QDs during the synthesis process forming the surface-capped OA and OLA long-chains [56]. The investigations showed that the surface-capped OA and OLA long-chains were removed after the treatment of SnO2 QDs by CuCl2 solution. The compositional analysis showed that most of the ligands were removed after the treatment at room temperature (RT). The removal of the ligand is more efficient when the treatment temperature is higher than 200 °C. In the meantime, the surface ligands can be removed at 600 °C without the ligand exchange treatment. However, the QDs can be agglomerated or sintered into the bulk structure at such high annealing temperatures [56]. Liu et al. reported the fabrication of SnO2 QDs based on the Tin(II) chloride dihydrate (SnCl2·2H2O) and ammonium thiocyanate (CH4N2S) containing precursor [57]. Their results also suggest that the hydrothermal treatment of the samples affects the size of QDs. Furthermore, it is possible to fabricate composite structures based on SnO2 QDs by adding carbon-containing suspensions to the precursor solution [58,59].

Colloidal TiO2 QDs were prepared using tetrabutyl titanate and acetic anhydride mixture [60]. The QDs were crystallized in the anatase phase at a relatively low temperature (80 °C). TiO2 QDs can be functionalized by using silver nitrate (AgNO3) as the precursor material [61]. The structural and compositional studies of Ag-functionalized QDs showed that the metallic Ag was placed on the surfaces of the TiO2 crystals indicating the successful functionalization of the structure. Unlike the pristine TiO2 [60], the crystallinity of functionalized QDs was higher and peaks of rutile TiO2 were also observed [61].

Pure and doped colloidal ZnO QDs can be prepared using zinc acetate (C4H6O4Zn) and zinc acetate dihydrate (C4H10O6Zn) containing precursors [62–64]. However, the annealing temperature of the ligand exchange procedure may cause the agglomeration of ZnO particles. Therefore, the thermal treatment procedure of QDs should be considered thoroughly to fabricate high quality structures [63,65]. ZnO QDs can be doped with other materials by changing the composition of the precursor solution. For example, indium-doped (In-doped) ZnO QDs were prepared by adding indium (III) chloride to the precursor solution [31]. The morphological and structural analysis of materials showed that the larger ion radius of In induced compressive stress in the crystal structure of ZnO decreasing the size of QDs from 5.5 nm to 3.7 (Fig. 3 ).

Fe3O4 QDs were fabricated using a two-step method [66]. In the first step, Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been prepared by hydrothermal treatment using ferric chloride and trisodium citrate containing ethylene glycol as a precursor solution. Then, Fe3O4 nanoparticles have been converted to the QDs by the thermal treatment procedure in 1.0 M ascorbic acid aqueous solution at 200 °C. Meanwhile, the size of particles decreased drastically from 250 to 2.5 nm.

The synthesis of colloidal chalcogenide QDs has been intensively developed along with the other semiconductor QDs. A wide range of metal chalcogenide QDs can be prepared using various precursors [29,53,67–69]. Particular attention should be paid to the precursor composition, synthesis temperature and processing time to prevent the segregation of particles [70]. The application of ligands is important to control the reactivity of precursors and the nucleation of QDs [70,71]. The colloidal chalcogenides can be synthesized without using specific substrates. Therefore, the colloidal method is very suitable for the coupling of chalcogenide QDs with metal oxide nanostructures [69,72,73]. However, Zhou et al. suggested that the deposition cycles of chalcogenide QDs on the metal oxide nanostructures need to be carefully considered [74]. They observed that the size and density of cadmium sulfide (CdS) QDs increased as a function of deposition cycles. Moreover, CdS QDs tend to agglomerate into bigger particles with increasing the number of deposition cycles.

Thus, the comparison of epitaxial and colloidal methods indicates that the epitaxial method is a precise technique for the growth of self-assembled QDs. Nevertheless, the major disadvantage of epitaxy is that the difference in the lattice structure and lattice constants between the substrate material and QDs limits the application of conventional substrates for gas sensing fabrication. As will be discussed further in Sections 3 (Tables 1 and 2
), the colloidal technique has been mainly used for the fabrication of metal oxide and chalcogenide QDs and their application in chemical gas sensors.

Carbon QDs have been prepared by bottom-up methods using a variety of carbon-containing precursors and surface passivating agents [75–80]. The green synthesis procedure of carbon QDs was carried out using apple juice as a precursor [81]. Spherical QDs with an average size of 4.5 nm were prepared. The development of green synthesis technologies is another important achievement to avoid the application of hazardous chemical materials. Carbon QDs with a similar diameter can be produced by the hydrothermal method using vitamin C as a precursor [75]. This study confirms the efficiency of the hydrothermal approach to fabricate low-dimensional carbon-containing materials.

The plasma treatment technique was applied for the preparation of carbon QDs with a very small diameter (1.8 nm) [76]. This synthesis method is carried out without complex purification procedures, where the isopropanol solution is the precursor. The plasma-generated electrons dissociate the isopropanol affecting the carbonization of the reactant. Therefore, the plasma power and the precursor concentrations are important parameters to control the composition and size of the resulting materials. Furthermore, the carbon QDs obtained by plasma treatment are amorphous [76].

The carbon QDs can also be prepared by thermal decomposition of citric acid, which is a very complex procedure including various intermediates that decompose at different temperatures [77]. Meanwhile, these reactions affect the functional properties of QDs. Carbon QDs with a homogeneous size distribution were synthesized by the impregnation method using mesoporous silica spheres as the nanoreactors and citric acid as a carbon precursor [82]. The silica spheres were impregnated with a mixed solution of complex salts and citric acid. Then, the carbon QDs were prepared by calcination and removal of silica spheres. It is important to note that the silica spheres not only determined the size distribution of QDs but also prevented their aggregation.

Several top-down approaches have been used for the synthesis of carbon QDs [83–86]. The chemical oxidation method was applied for producing QDs using commercially activated carbon as the source material [83]. After the treatment with nitric acid, the activated carbon with the amorphous structure was etched into individual CDs. The electrochemical oxidation was also applied for the synthesis of carbon QDs [87]. The process was carried out in a two-electrode cell, where the graphite rods were used as the anode and counter electrode. The exfoliation of carbon was performed by the application of static potential. After the electrochemical procedure, the aqueous solution was filtered and centrifuged to remove the precipitated graphite oxide and graphite particles. The resulting solution was carbon QD-containing water (H2O). Meanwhile, composite structures based on carbon QDs and metal oxides can be fabricated by the electrochemical oxidation method adding metal precursors in the solution [87].

Another typical synthesis method of carbon QDs is the laser ablation, where the structures can be prepared by laser irradiation of a carbon target. Therefore, the laser power and the distance between the focusing lens and carbon target are the key parameters to control the size and distribution of QDs [85]. Carbon QDs with a homogeneous size distribution were produced by performing an ultrasonic reaction between the glucose and ammonium hydroxide [88]. Further, it was demonstrated that food waste can be used as a precursor to fabricate carbon QDs using ultrasound irradiation [86,89]. The dimension of QDs was varied from 1 to 7 nm (Fig. 4 ). Moreover, the post-growth treatment of structures by nitric acid affected the formation of oxygen-containing groups on their surface, which is an important feature to modify the functionalities of prepared materials [86].

There are two types of gas adsorption on the surface of semiconductors: (i) physical (physisorption) and (ii) chemical (chemisorption). Physisorption is a weak dipolar interaction between the gas molecules and solid materials, which is driven by van der Waals forces. The heat released from the physisorption is less than 6 kcal/mol. Instead, chemisorption is a strong interaction of gas molecules with solids, which results to the charge transfer between gaseous species and the solid surface. The heat of chemisorption is greater than 15 kcal/mol. Thus, physisorption process reduces with increasing temperature while chemisorption enhances. Therefore, physisorption may transform into chemisorption at elevated temperatures [90].

The main component of air is oxygen. Therefore, the interaction of semiconductor materials with oxygen plays a crucial role to identify the presence of other gases in the environment [90]. The chemical gas sensors operate at the temperature range of RT to 500 °C [91,92]. At these temperatures, oxygen can be ionosorbed on the semiconductor in molecular (
O 2 −
) and atomic (
O −
) forms trapping surface electrons (Eq. (2) and Eq. (3) [90]). The
O 2 −
ions dominate on the sensing material at the operating temperatures below 150 °C, while the
O −
atomic forms dominate at the temperatures higher than 150 °C [90,91].

Thus, the ionosorbed oxygen species extract the electrons from the conduction band of the n-type semiconductor material resulting to the formation of a depletion layer and a Schottky barrier (
q

) at the grain boundaries (Fig. 5 (a)). Consequently, the conductance of the structure will be decreased. The height of the Schottky barrier and the depletion width (

G r

The width of depletion layer can be expressed as a function of the Debye length (

Thus, the oxidizing gases act as acceptors upon adsorption on the surface of n-type semiconductor materials, while the reducing gases act as electron donors. In n-type semiconductor gas sensors, the former reduces the electrical conductance of the structure and the latter leads to an increase in the conductance. The oxidizing and reducing gases cause similar changes in the electrical conductance of p-type gas sensors but in the opposite direction [90,92]. Thus, the target gases will be detected by measuring the conductance changes of the semiconductor material depending on its interaction with oxidizing or reducing agents. Moreover, when the particle size is comparable to quantum dimensions, it will be completely depleted by charge carriers, which is beneficial to improve the sensing response of the material [94].

The chemiresistive structures for gas sensing applications consist of the substrate, sensing layer, heater and electrodes. The heater and the electrodes are used to control the temperature of the sensing layer and measure its conductance. Fig. 5 (b) shows the configuration of a typical chemiresistive gas sensor. As has already been mentioned in section 2.2, the QDs integrated into the sensing structures have been mainly synthesized by colloidal methods. Therefore, the dispersions of QDs were prepared using different solutions. Then, the materials were deposited on the substrates (before or after the electrode deposition) using drop-casting, spin-coating, spray-coating and screen-printing techniques. Afterward, the sensors were thermally treated to remove the residual compounds and stabilize their electrical parameters [31,58,67,95–97].

Metal oxides have been considered to be the most studied materials for applications in conductometric sensors due to their good chemical stability and functional properties [92,98–102]. Therefore, the scientific community of gas sensors has been greatly motivated to develop novel oxide structures in nanoscale and explore new features of these materials [103–108]. Table 1 summarizes the achievements in the field of chemiresistive sensors based on metal oxide QDs. The enhancement in the sensing response of SnO2 QDs compared to the conventional SnO2 sensors was reported in one of the pioneering works [94]. The diameter of the fabricated SnO2 QDs was about 3–4 nm. The Eg of the structure was 5.7 eV, which is larger than that of the bulk SnO2 (3.6 eV). The SnO2 QDs showed about three times higher sensing response towards CO compared to the conventional SnO2 gas sensors (Table 1). The significant enhancement in response was attributed to the reduced dimensions of structure, where the
L D
of SnO2 played a crucial role (Fig. 6 [94]). The size of prepared QDs was close to twice the
L D
of SnO2 (3 nm) at 250 °C, and therefore the response value of the sensor towards 1000 ppm of CO increased from 27 to 147 at a relatively low operating temperature range (50–225 °C). Moreover, the surface reactivity of SnO2 particles was enhanced with the increase in the surface-to-bulk ratio. The presence of active oxygen species on the surface of SnO2 has a significant effect on its interaction with reducing gases. The increase in SnO2 surface area provides more surface defects and oxygen vacancies enhancing the adsorption of CO [109].

The thermal treatment of as-prepared colloidal SnO2 QDs to remove the organic ligands may further improve the charge transfer in the sensing structure and its interaction with the target gas at low operating temperatures. After the ligand exchange procedure, SnO2 QDs with a diameter of 2–3 nm showed a high response towards hydrogen sulfide (H2S) at an operating temperature of 70 °C [110]. The response and recovery times of the sensor were 37 and 127 s, respectively. SnO2 QDs showed reproducible functional performance after the 5 cycles of measurements at the same conditions with a 6 ppb of the limit of detection (LOD), which is an important result for the manufacturing of high precision gas sensing devices. The comparison of SnO2 QDs [95,110] and recently developed H2S sensors based on the SnO2 nanowires and nanograins with bigger diameters shows that the sensing performance of QDs is still better [111,112]. Besides, the operating temperature of H2S sensors based on SnO2 QDs is lower (70 °C) than that of other structures (80–350 °C [111,112]). This is important parameter to reduce the power consumption of the detection systems. Hence, these findings confirm the beneficial effect of particle size reduction to the quantum dimensions considering their completely depletion by charge carriers and the increasing number of surface oxygen species [94,113]. However, the experimental findings of Liu et al. showed that the thermal treatment of SnO2 QDs can cause changes in their size [57]. The average size of as-prepared SnO2 QDs (1.9 nm) increased to 2.7 and 4 nm after the thermal treatment procedure at 125 and 225 °C, respectively. Meanwhile, the response of the fabricated sensor towards hydrogen (H2) was changed due to the variation in the size of QDs. It should be noted that the Eg of QDs can be tuned depending on their size [11]. Metal oxide chemical sensors mainly operate at relatively high temperatures [92,114]. Therefore, to obtain reproducible sensing performance the electrical parameters of the gas sensors based on metal oxide QDs should be stabilized before the gas sensing measurements considering the variations of the dot size as a function of treatment/working temperature.

To overcome the charge transport barrier in SnO2 QDs is possible by coupling them with high carrier mobility structures. The multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with their excellent charge transport properties were incorporated in the SnO2 QD matrix [95]. SnO2 QDs/MWCNTs hybrid structures were obtained by mixing the SnO2 toluene solution and MWCNTs chloroform suspension with each other using the magnetic stirring method. Then, the prepared suspension was deposited on the substrates by the spin-coating method. A synergetic effect between the SnO2 QDs and the MWCNTs was achieved for the detection of H2S. The authors suggested that SnO2 QDs were the key sensing structure, where the MWCNT enhanced charge transport properties of the sensors. They assumed that due to the energy band alignment at the SnO2 QDs/MWCNT interfaces, MWCNTs may accept the electrons that are injected from H2S to QDs. Thus, the MWCNTs will act as charge transport pathways enhancing the electrical and sensing properties of QDs. The composite structures showed an excellent and selective response towards H2S at 70 °C. The response value of SnO2 QDs/MWCNTs sensor was about three times higher than that of pure SnO2 (Table 1). Meanwhile, the functionalities of the prepared composites were compared to the similar structures reported in literature [95]. The SnO2 QDs/MWCNTs sensors showed 1 order of magnitude higher response in respect to the other sensing devices operating from RT to 150 °C. Moreover, the composite materials were integrated into the transducer by spin-coating approach without using high vacuum conditions and expensive deposition technologies. The developed easy fabrication technique and relatively low operating temperature are very attractive for the manufacturing of portable and inexpensive monitoring systems.

The structural, compositional and morphological modifications of carbon-based materials may alter the sensing performance of SnO2 QDs [58,59]. The effect of MWCNTs and carbon nanohorns (CNHs) on the sensing properties of SnO2 QDs was investigated in the presence of hydrocarbon gases [58]. The structures were prepared by mixing the CNTs or CNHs aqueous dispersion with SnCl4·5H2O aqueous solution followed by hydrothermal treatment. The response and selectivity of SnO2 QDs/MWCNTs and SnO2 QDs/CNHs composites towards methane (CH4), ethane (C2H6) and propane (C3H8) were varied depending on their working temperature. However, the composite structures based on SnO2 QDs and carbon-containing materials show better sensing characteristics towards H2S compared to the other gases (Table 1). Therefore, these composites are more preferable for the manufacturing of H2S sensing devices. In the meantime, they show reproducible sensing performance at different environmental conditions. For example, a hybrid material based on SnO2 QDs and fullerene C60 (SnO2 QDs–C60) was tested towards hydrocarbon gases and H2S [59]. The experimental analysis showed that the SnO2 QDs–C60 material has significantly stronger and reversible interaction with the H2S compared to the CH4 and C3H8. The interaction between the gases and the surface of the sensing material was investigated using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The interaction between the aforementioned gases and the surface of the sensing material was investigated using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The results showed that H2S has more negative interaction energy with the material than CH4 and C3H8. Furthermore, the CH4 and C3H8 had similar contribution on band gap and conductivity of SnO2 QDs–C60, which was significantly weaker compared to the H2S effect. The obtained results were in agreement with the experimental studies confirming the selectivity of the SnO2 QDs–C60 to H2S. The sensing measurements of the SnO2 QDs–C60 composite were carried out for different times and the structure showed reproducible functional properties. The response value of SnO2 QDs–C60 sensor was varied of about 10% when the relative humidity (RH) in the test chamber was increased from 0 to 75%. This is an important achievement for the application of the final sensing device in normal ambient conditions. Besides, this study confirmed the previously obtained results on the improvement of H2S sensing properties of SnO2 QDs due to their coupling with carbon-containing structures, where the latter enhance the charge transport properties of QDs (Table 1).

The carbon-based materials showed a p-type gas sensing behavior [115–118]. Instead, the aforementioned investigations indicate that the response-type of composites is dominated by the n-type metal oxide (Fig. 7 ), where the carbon-containing structure affects the charge transport improving the sensing performance of metal oxides [58,59,95].

The treatment of the SnO2 QDs using CuCl2 may remove the surface ligands maintaining the QC of the structure. Moreover, the CuCl2 forms p-CuO/n-SnO2 heterojunctions improving the sensing performance of QDs. The response of as-prepared SnO2 QDs towards 50 ppm of H2S was increased from 414 to 1755 after the ligand exchange treatment using CuCl2 [56]. The treated heterostructures showed reproducible sensing results after several measurements.

The ligand exchange procedure also improved the functionalities of the colloidal ZnO QDs (Fig. 8 [65]). The treatment of surface ligands enhanced the charge carrier transport properties in QDs and improved the interaction of structure with the H2S at RT. As a result, the response of the treated ZnO QDs (113.5) was significantly higher compared to as-prepared materials (1.07). However, the H2O molecules affected the resistance of QDs at RT and their sensing response partially decreased when the RH increased.

As can be seen in Table 1, metal oxide QDs showed a high response towards H2S at relatively low operating temperatures when the concentration of the analyte is ≥ 50 ppm. In addition, the LOD of H2S sensors is mostly unavailable. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the occupational exposure limit of H2S is around 10 ppm [119]. Consequently, the sensing response of metal oxide QDs should be further improved for their integration in H2S monitoring systems.

To observe the quantum size effect in a semiconductor material its crystal size should be on the same order as the exciton Bohr radius (the distance between the electron-hole pair) [120]. Therefore, to optimize the performance of gas sensors based on semiconductor QDs the exciton Bohr radius can be considered [63]. The sensing parameters of ZnO QDs with the size close to the exciton Bohr radius (2.34 nm [121]) were tested towards H2, ammonia (NH3), sulfur dioxide (SO2) and organic compounds at the temperature range of RT‒175 °C. The structures showed a selective response towards H2 with respect to other gases at an operating temperature of 175 °C. The response value of the fabricated sensors towards 4000 ppm of H2 was 0.8. The flammability limit of H2 is 4000 ppm [122]. Hence, further improvement of the sensing performance of ZnO QDs is needed.

Doping may lead to an enhancement in the sensitivity of ZnO QDs. For example, the effect of aluminum (Al) doping on the sensing properties of ZnO QDs has been described [62]. Incorporation of Al increased the concentration of oxygen vacancies in ZnO QDs improving their response towards 2-chloroethyl ethyl sulfide (2-CEES) [62]. Al–ZnO QDs exhibited a response value of 5393 towards 20 ppm of 2-CEES, which is significantly higher compared to the previously reported 2-CEES chemiresistive sensors [62]. Moreover, DFT calculations showed that Al doping may affect the band gap and density of states of ZnO leading to the enhancement in the conductivity and functionalities of the material [123]. A similar effect was observed for the In-doped ZnO (In–ZnO) QDs [31]. In this case, the response of In–ZnO structure towards 10 ppm of acetylene (C2H2) was drastically enhanced compared to the pristine QDs (from 2.7 to 19.3). Thus, the choice of dopant material is a critical factor for the improvement of the sensing performance of metal oxide QDs towards a specific gas.

ZnO QDs were also used in the fabrication of gas sensors based on composite structures. Wu et al. functionalized SnO2 nanosheets by ZnO QDs [64]. The QDs increased the surface area of SnO2 nanosheets improving the adsorption of nitrogen dioxide (NO2) on the structure at RT. Meanwhile, the Zn:Sn molar ratio played an important role in gas sensing performance of the composite material. The optimum Zn:Sn ratio was 1:100. Then, the response of fabricated sensors was tested under ultraviolet irradiation (UV-irradiation). The obtained results showed that the response value of ZnO QDs‒SnO2 towards NO2 was about three times higher than that of the pristine SnO2 nanosheets. The experimental studies indicated that UV-activation and absorbed photon energy lead to the excitation of electrons resulting in an increase of the density of free charge carriers and a modulation in the height of the potential barrier at grain boundaries of semiconductor material improving its functional properties [21,124,125]. Hence, the application of UV light is an efficient strategy to enhance the sensing response of metal oxide QDs at RT. Meanwhile, the reduction of the band gap of semiconductor materials will allow the application of photoactivation tools having lower energy. The band gap and thus the functionalities of QDs can be varied depending on their size [9–11]. Therefore, QDs with different sizes should be prepared in future experimental works and the photoactivation effect on the performance of chemiresistive sensors depending on the dot size should be investigated. Moreover, Wu and co-authors reported that formation of a heterojunction between different oxide materials promotes the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, which is another advantage for the development of composite structures and the application of photoactivation method [64].

TiO2 QDs have been successfully applied in the fabrication of chemiresistive gas sensors [60,61]. The experimental findings indicated that the size reduction of TiO2 particles (2–5 nm) significantly affected the operating temperature of sensors. The prepared QDs showed a high and selective response towards NH3 at RT. Their response value towards 10 ppm of NH3 was 7.8. This achievement is important for the manufacturing of low power consumption detection systems based on TiO2. The functionalization of TiO2 QDs with Ag can alter their sensing performance. Ag acts as a catalyst and the contact potential barrier of the material is decreased enhancing the adsorption of NH3 at RT [61]. The response of functionalized TiO2 QDs (Ag–TiO2) towards 10 ppm of NH3 was three times higher compared to the pure TiO2 (Table 1 [60,61]). Moreover, the sensing properties of Ag–TiO2 material were changed as a function of Ag:Ti molar ratio. Long-term stability of the pristine and functionalized TiO2 QDs were examined for 45 days. The structures showed reproducible sensing performance [60,61]. Additionally, it has been reported that the toxic effects will be observed following exposure to 50 ppm of NH3 [126]. Hence, the excellent sensing performance of TiO2 QD-based sensors at RT indicates that the application of QDs has a great potential to overcome the limitations of thin film sensing devices such as the high operating temperature and relatively low sensitivity [127–129]. In addition, the size reduction of TiO2 to the QD scale resulted in better sensing performance of material compared to the thin film structures of TiO2 functionalized with catalytic layers or coupled with other metal oxide materials [127,128]. These findings suggest that future investigations should be performed on the band gap versus QD size dependence of TiO2 to tailor its sensing properties towards other gases.

Furthermore, the toxicity studies of several metal oxide nanomaterials confirmed that they can be used in the fabrication of gas sensors without adverse health effects [130–132]. Overall, the experimental studies reported to date indicate that the operating temperature of metal oxide gas sensors comprising QD has been relatively decreased compared with conventional conductometric sensors (Table 1). When integrating the metal oxide QDs into the design of chemiresistive sensors, they may decrease the power consumption, and therefore the size of detection systems. However, the reduction of the operating temperature of sensors should be considered in parallel to the humidity effect on the functionalities of QDs. H2O vapor may affect the hydroxylation of the surface of sensing material at relatively low operating temperatures (Eq. (10)), where
O
H −
groups are attracted to the cations of the oxide and
H +
protons are attracted to the oxide ions. Thus, the hydroxyl groups can passivate the adsorption sites on the sensing structure, while the
H +
protons can form OH groups interacting with the oxygen species [114,133]. Hence, the sensing properties of the semiconductor QDs can be affected by the adsorption and desorption of H2O molecules. In particular, the RH changes may have a significant effect on the performance of QDs at RT [65,134]. The surface of sensing materials will be dehydroxylated more intensively with increasing their operating temperature [63]. Meanwhile, the synergy effect in composite structures may stabilize their functionalities at different levels of RH [59]. However, most of the gas sensing tests of metal oxide QDs have been carried out at a constant concentration of RH (Table 1). Instead, for practical applications, the functionalities of QD-based sensors need to be investigated depending on the concentration of RH. Therefore, further studies should be devoted to examine the gas sensing properties of QDs-based structures with different compositions under variation of RH.

Meanwhile, it is worth mentioning that the long-term stability and reproducibility of most of the sensors based on metal oxide QDs should be further studied to evaluate their possible applications in real-time monitoring systems (Table 1).

Metal chalcogenides have been used to develop functional devices due to their excellent catalytic and electrical properties [30,135–139]. The surface modification and the size reduction of metal chalcogenides to the QD scale improved their chemical and physical performances [140–142].

The gas sensing parameters of chalcogenide QD-containing structures are listed in Table 2. Materials and processing strategies for the treatment of PbS colloidal QDs have a crucial effect on their sensing characteristics. As-prepared PbS QDs were not sensitive towards NO2 at RT. However, after the treatment of QDs using sodium nitrite (NaNO2), the sensors showed a very high response (21.7) towards 50 ppm of NO2 [22]. The experimental analyses confirmed that the surface ligands hinder the effective interaction between the PbS and NO2. Besides, the response of treated QDs was changed depending on the Pb:S ratio. The sensing structures exhibited an optimal response at the Pb:S ratio of 4:1. In the meantime, the LOD of sensors was 84 ppb. DFT simulations were performed to study the NO2 gas sensing mechanism of the fabricated sensors. The binding energy for NO2 on PbS was much stronger (0.8 eV) than that of oxygen (0.5 eV) indicating that NO2 molecules may readily replace the oxygen bonded to QDs. Moreover, unlike the strong covalent binding between the OA ligand and PbS, the estimated binding energy of NO2 (0.8 eV) was 4 times weaker confirming its reversible interaction with the sensing structure. The functional properties of treated QDs were reproducible after the 5 cycles of gas sensing measurements at the same conditions. The deposition procedure of PbS colloidal QDs on the substrates also affects their reaction with NO2 (Fig. 9 ) as the porosity and functional performance of the sensing layers are changed depending on the solvent composition, concentration and deposition technique [67].

PbS is a p-type semiconductor [143]. However, Li et al. observed that the response of PbS QDs was turned from p-to n-type due to the exposure to H2S at the elevated temperatures (50–135 °C) [144]. Their findings suggested that the response transition effect at high operating temperatures can be associated with the reactions between the H2S and the oxygen-related states on the surface of PbS QDs. They also proposed that the transition of the sensing response from p-to n-type may occur due to the formation of additional energy levels at the edge of the conduction band of PbS QDs. Nevertheless, comprehensive investigations were not carried out to confirm these hypotheses.

Outputs of the studies on gas sensing characteristics of PbS QDs indicate that they are sensitive towards NO2 mainly at RT. Moreover, their LOD values are a few tens of ppb (Table 2). Instead, NO2 gas sensors based on PbS thin films exhibited relatively low sensing response at higher operating temperature (150 °C [145]). Therefore, PbS QDs are more attractive structures for applications in NO2 monitoring systems. However, the experimental analyses were carried under exposure to a few tens of ppm of NO2. Meanwhile, adverse health effects have been observed at NO2 concentrations of 1.5–2 ppm [146]. Therefore, more experimental analyses are needed to evaluate their possible application in real-life conditions.

Pure and functionalized tungsten disulfide (WS2) QDs were employed in the detection of carbon dioxide (CO2) [147]. Functionalization of WS2 QDs with ruthenium (Ru) partially improved the response of pristine QDs (Table 2). The humidity changes in the test chamber affected the response of WS2 QDs due to the formation of hydroxyl radicals on their surface. Instead, significant variations in the response values of Ru-functionalized QDs were not observed at different levels of RH. Thus, Ru inhibited the formation of hydroxyl radicals on the sensors. Furthermore, slight changes in the gas sensing response of the pristine WS2 QDs ware observed after the long-term stability analysis, while Ru-functionalized QDs exhibited very stable sensing performance. The outdoor CO2 concentration should be below 400 ppm, while the elevated levels of CO2 (≥500 ppm) may affect human health [148]. In terms of stabilization, Ru played an important role in the functional properties of WS2 QDs at different concentrations of ambient humidity, however, their sensing response is relatively poor (see Table 2) and should be enhanced for the applications in CO2 monitoring.

Metal chalcogenides QDs can also be used in composite structures to improve their electrical and sensing properties. For example, the formation of a depletion layer between the p-type PbS QDs and the n-type TiO2 nanotubes resulted in a concentration increase of active sites in the sensing structure improving the adsorption of gaseous compounds on its surface [73]. The composite material exhibited a selective response towards 100 ppm of NH3 at RT. In addition, the PbS QDs improved the charge transport through the TiO2 nanotube arrays affecting their gas sensing characteristics. However, considering the exposure limit value (10 ppm) of NH3 [126], the sensing performance of the prepared composite structure should be further enhanced. Zhang et al. examined the effect of PbS QDs on the sensing properties of alpha-iron oxide/polyaniline (α-Fe2O3/PANI) composite at RT [72]. The PbS QDs enlarged the depletion layer width of composite material creating more active states. Therefore, the response value of α-Fe2O3/PANI structure towards methanol (CH3OH) increased from 87.5 to 135. The electrical and sensing characteristics of the QD-containing α-Fe2O3/PANI material were reproducible after several exposure cycles to CH3OH. This study confirms the feasibility of the application of QDs to improve the electrical and gas sensing properties of composite structures.

CdS QDs with their large specific surface area and good charge transport properties affected the sensing performance of Co3O4 microspheres towards H2S [68]. The CdS/Co3O4 composites showed high sensing response as well as very short response and recovery times at 25 °C (see Table 2). Small fluctuations in the response values of composite material were observed over the 30 days of measurements at 25 °C. However, the CdS QDs can be oxidized and decomposed into CdO at the operating temperatures higher than 25 °C [68]. Therefore, the chemical stability of CdS QDs should be improved to reduce the temperature effect on their functionalities. The results from other studies on composite structures based on CdSe and metal oxides (ZnO, SnO2 and In2O3) indicated that their properties can be further improved by applying green light irradiation [69,149]. This is an effective strategy for the fabrication of NO2 gas sensors. Under green light irradiation, the electron-hole pairs will be generated in the CdSe QDs and not in the wide band gap oxide materials, where UV activation is needed. Hence, the photoactivated electrons in the CdSe will move to the conduction band of the metal oxide matrix, whereas the holes will recombine with the electrons trapped by the chemisorbed NO2. The composites materials based on CdSe QDs and metal oxides exhibited reproducible sensing characteristics in dark and under green light activation [69,149]. Furthermore, the CdSe QDs‒In2O3 structure showed a better sensing response towards NO2 compared to other compositions, which was attributed to the maximum band offset between the In2O3 and CdSe QDs [69].

As can be seen in Table 2, the sensors comprising metal chalcogenide QDs mainly operate at RT. This is an advantage for the fabrication of low power consumption gas sensors. However, unlike the metal oxide gas sensors, the chalcogenides are relatively new materials in gas sensing devices. Therefore, the effects of environmental conditions on their performance should be further investigated.

Song et al. developed a promising approach for the fabrication of flexible gas sensors based PbS QDs [29]. They enhanced the response of QDs towards NO2 at RT and reduced the humidity effect on sensors controlling the strain of flexible substrates. Furthermore, the hydrophobic long-chains of OA and OLA capped the surface of PbS QDs reducing the adsorption of H2O molecules. The sensors showed reproducible functional properties with a short response and recovery times and very low LOD (13 ppb). These results indicate that the type of substrate material, the OA and OLA long-chains may alter the hydrophobicity of the sensors based on chalcogenide QDs. Therefore, these approaches can be applied to stabilize the functionalities of the sensors based on chalcogenide QDs.

The coupling of p-type CdTe QDs with the n-type MoS2 nanowires (CdTe QDs/MoS2) can alter their sensing characteristics towards NO2 at RT [30]. CdTe QDs with a diameter of 3–4 nm enhanced the specific surface area of pristine MoS2 nanowires and improved their electrical properties. The formation of an energy barrier at the p-n heterojunction and its further modulation due to the adsorption/desorption processes of NO2 played a significant role in the sensing performance of the structure (Fig. 10 ). The response of CdTe QDs/MoS2 towards NO2 at 80% of RH was reduced to about 6% compared to the response value recorded in dry air, which was attributed to the hydrophobicity of the composite material. Moreover, the sensor exhibited short response and recovery times (16/114 s) at RT. This study demonstrates that taking advantage of the coupling of p-type semiconductor QDs with n-type transition metal dichalcogenides is a promising way to develop high performance gas sensors.

The aforementioned investigations indicate that the semiconductor chalcogenides are attractive materials for gas sensing applications. However, some chalcogenide QDs (Cd, S, Se, and Te-containing) are cytotoxic [150–153]. Therefore, more research should be done to determine the toxicity effects of semiconductor chalcogenides during their fabrication process and application in gas sensing systems.

Carbon QDs with their unique electronic properties have attracted growing interest in gas sensing applications [97,154]. However, significant and reversible variations in the electrical conductance of graphene QDs as a function of RH were observed [155,156]. These changes can be attributed to the depletion region formed between the QDs and H2O molecules. Hence, the sensing mechanism of graphene QDs can be associated with the capillary condensation of H2O molecules on the surface of dots, where the conductive H2O path dominates the resistance of the circuit and the current density changes depending on the concentration of RH [155]. Conductometric sensors based on graphene QDs were used to measure the moisture content in bentonite and white clay soils (Fig. 11 [156]). The presence of different functional groups (epoxide, carboxyl and hydroxyl) in the graphene QDs was crucial for the physisorption process of H2O molecules on their surface. In addition, the Grotthuss chain reaction (the diffusion of the protons through the networks formed by H2O molecules, Eq. (11) [157,158]) and ionic conductivity affected the sensitivity of graphene QDs at high moisture contents [156]. The response time of the sensors was varied from 120 to 180 s, which is quite fast for agriculture-related applications. The long-term stability of graphene QDs was examined over the 4 months. The experiments showed no changes in the sensing performance of structures.

Further studies indicated that the conductance change of graphene QDs at the higher concentrations of RH (52–97%) is attributed to the proton transport via the Grotthuss mechanism [96]. While at low concentrations of RH (0–52%), the adsorption of H2O molecules on QDs may be rather promoted by the negatively charged carboxyl groups than the hydroxyl functional groups of graphene. In addition, the application of flexible sensors based on graphene QDs to identify the level of RH was very successful [159]. The sensors showed a high response (390) at 99% of RH with a short response and recovery times (12 and 43 s, respectively). These experimental studies indicate that the graphene QDs can be considered for the fabrication of humidity sensors.

The chemical composition of functional groups in graphene QDs plays a crucial role in their sensing response towards different gaseous compounds [97,160]. The acidic and neutral graphene QDs exhibited significantly different sensing performance towards NH3 at RT [97]. When the acidic QDs are exposed to NH3 in the presence of high RH (35–80%), the carboxyl groups on graphene are unprotonated increasing the conductance of the sensors. Hence, the physisorbed H2O molecules have a huge effect on the sensing performance of QDs. As a result, the NH3 molecules dominate in the conductance changes of acidic QDs at low levels of RH (<35%). Instead, the neutral graphene QDs are not sensitive to humidity changes due to their hydrophobic nature [160]. In this case, the NH3 molecules adsorb on the surface of QDs acting as electron donors and change the conductance of the structure [97].

In comparison with the pristine graphene, the graphene oxide is a strongly oxygenated form of the graphene that contains different functional groups [116]. The physisorbed H2O molecules on graphene oxide QDs can be ionized under electrostatic field creating hydronium ions as charge carriers. Wherein, the charge transport proceeds through the Grotthuss chain reaction (Eq. (7)). Moreover, the intersection density inside the small size QDs (<10 nm) is higher compared to the conventional graphene oxide films, providing a better permeation of H2O molecules. Therefore, the graphene oxide QDs showed high sensitivity to the changes of RH with a very short response and recovery times (140 and 204 ms, respectively) [158].

The partial reduction of graphene oxide may improve its conductivity and gas sensing response [115,116,118,165]. The reduction of as-prepared graphene oxide QDs increased their response towards 5 ppm of hydrogen cyanide (HCN) by an order of magnitude. The LOD of the fabricated sensors was 600 ppb at RT [166]. However, the response of the reduced graphene oxide QDs was dramatically decreased when the concentration of RH in the test chamber was higher than 45%. This study also confirms that the high levels of RH have a huge effect on the sensing performance of carbon QDs.

The overall research findings suggest that the development of gas sensors based on carbon QDs is still in its early stages. The preliminary results for the detection of explosive and toxic gaseous compounds indicate that they are inferior to conventional semiconductor gas sensors (Table 3). The sensing properties of carbon QDs need to be extensively explored at relatively high operating temperatures considering their stability at different levels of RH. However, the carbon QDs are highly biocompatible, which makes them very attractive materials for application in functional devices [81].

Boron nitride (BN) exists in two main forms: (i) hexagonal crystal structure with properties similar to graphite, (ii) cubic crystalline structure with properties similar to diamond [167]. The functionalization of ZnO nanoparticles with BN QDs enhanced their response towards 100 ppm of xylene and toluene more than three times [168]. The morphological analysis of samples showed that the surface area of pure ZnO nanomaterial was larger with respect to the BN QDs‒ZnO composite. However, BN QDs acted as catalysts generating more oxygen species and active sites for the adsorption of gases on the surface of sensor. The effect of humidity on the sensing performance of the materials was reduced by increasing their operating temperature. However, this strategy will increase the power consumption of chemiresistive sensors. The reliability of the BN QDs‒ZnO sensors was tested for a period of 50 days at an operating temperature of 370 °C. The composites showed stable electrical properties indicating the potential of BN QDs to improve the functional performance of metal oxide nanomaterials.

The great interest in semiconductor QDs for chemical gas sensing applications is evident from the research studies considered in this manuscript. Two main approaches (epitaxial and colloidal) have been developed for the preparation of QDs. The epitaxial growth method allows precise control of the size and shape of QDs ensuring the reproducibility of the preparation procedure. Meanwhile, many issues on the homogeneity and quality of epitaxial QDs have been addressed. However, despite the progress in the epitaxial synthesis technique, the equipment and technological procedures for the preparation of QDs are expensive. Furthermore, the orientation, the compositional and structural parameters of the substrate material are the key factors of the epitaxial synthesis procedure, which limits the application of low-cost polycrystalline and polymer substrates in the architecture of sensing devices. Therefore, the researchers have intensively developed the colloidal method trying to reduce the complexity and the cost of QD preparation procedures. This approach enables the fabrication of semiconductor QDs in liquid-phase providing shape and size control of the structures. In this case, the precursor solution, the process temperature and duration greatly influence the growth process. Nevertheless, the synthesis of doped and complex structures based on the semiconductor QDs is still in its infancy. More investigations should be performed, considering different precursor solutions and growth regimes to fabricate a wide range of doped and composite materials.

The chemical nature of QDs is very important for their gas sensing characteristics. The formation of ligands on the surface of as-prepared QDs hinders the interaction between the QDs and gaseous compounds. This issue can be overcome by the surface treatment of QDs. The research findings also indicate that the treatment of as-prepared materials improves their charge carrier transport. However, the ligand exchange treatment and the annealing of QDs should be considered in detail depending on the type of the material. Because the presence of ligands improves the humidity resistance of QDs, which is an important parameter for chemical sensors. Furthermore, the thermal treatment of as-prepared QDs and the stabilization of their particle size can be performed to fabricate gas sensors with reliable sensing performance.

The most research on metal oxide gas sensors has been focused on the SnO2, ZnO and TiO2 QDs. These structures have shown good sensitivity to the hydrogen-containing gases. In the meantime, metal oxides are very attractive materials for gas sensing applications due to their chemical stability and non-toxic nature. Therefore, the QDs based on other oxide materials should be explored to detect different gaseous compounds.

The chalcogenides are relatively new materials in chemical gas sensing application compared to the metal oxides. The metal chalcogenide QDs have shown promising sensing properties mainly at RT, which is favorable for the development of low powers consumption gas sensing systems. Meanwhile, some studies indicate that Cd, S, Se, and Te-containing chalcogenides are cytotoxic. Further work is required to determine which kind of chalcogenide QDs can be safely used in the fabrication of chemical gas sensors.

In this regard, the analysis of carbon QDs showed that they are biocompatible materials. Several studies have examined the gas sensing performance of carbon QDs. The presence of epoxide, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in the carbon QDs has a crucial effect on their sensing characteristics. The modifications in the composition of carbon QDs depending on their reduction at different treatment temperatures should be thoroughly investigated to optimize their sensing parameters. The studies seem to suggest that humidity is a significant factor influencing the sensing behavior of carbon QDs. Still, more work needs to be done on this topic, as humidity sensing is an important area for the application of conductometric sensors.

Overall, the reduction of the particle size to quantum dimensions will provide its complete depletion by charge carriers, which is a crucial factor to achieve high sensitivity. Therefore, the QDs seem more preferable to other structures for the fabrication of high performance gas sensors. However, such small particles are not stable at high operating temperatures. In the meantime, some experimental studies show that the size reduction of particles (comparable to their exciton Bohr radius) significantly decreased operating temperature of gas sensors. Thus, future investigations in this filed considering the stability and the sensitivity improvement of QDs will give a clear idea to evaluate their application in gas sensing devices.

The enhancement of selectivity still remains one of the major concerns of QDs. The electronic band structure of QDs can be modified depending on their size. The operating temperature and the band gap of the semiconductor material affect its interaction with different gaseous compounds. Therefore, the modulation of the band gap of QDs by varying their size can be an additional factor to improve the sensing performance of gas sensors. However, this kind of analyses have not been carried out yet and need to be performed in future studies.

The doping and functionalization of QDs may have a beneficial effect on their response and selectivity. The experimental results indicate that it is important to find out the optimum concentration of each dopant material in the structure for the detection of a specific gas in the environment.

The integration of QDs in the polymer matrix and semiconductor composites has proven to be an efficient strategy to improve their charge transport and interaction with gaseous compounds leading to the enhanced sensing response. Progress in the studies of the concentration effect of QDs on the sensing performance of composites is still ongoing.

The coupling of the p-type QDs with the n-type materials offers additional possibilities to tailor the properties of semiconductor gas sensors. The research findings suggest that the formation of an energy barrier at the p-n heterojunction and its modulation due to the adsorption/desorption of gaseous compounds play a critical role in the properties of chemiresistive sensors.

Considering the aforementioned results in the studies of QDs for their application in chemiresistive devices, one can expect that the QC effect opens the door to new classes of semiconductor materials that have not been used in the production of gas sensing devices. Meanwhile, the outstanding functional features of QDs may lead to significant advances in the development of low power consumption and small size monitoring systems. However, it should be noted that the QD-based sensors are only beginning to be integrated into conductometric gas sensing systems. The effect of synthesis procedures on the properties of QDs, the stability and durability of QD-based gas sensors at different levels of RH have been examined in a few studies. These are critical parameters in the monitoring and diagnostic technologies and need to be investigated in future works. Moreover, the fabrication of these next-generation sensing devices based on semiconductor QDs requires a combination of synthesis methods of materials and large-scale production technologies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This paper is dedicated to the memory of Dr. Valeri M. Arakelyan from Yerevan State University who passed away recently. He was a brilliant scientist, a wonderful person and a family man. Dr. V. M. Arakelyan was working on the development of photoelectrochemical converters and gas sensing systems achieving appreciable results in the aforementioned fields.
I also would like to thank the healthcare workers of Italy fighting COVID-19 to ensure our health and achieve a safe work environment during this challenging time.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Vardan Galstyan got his Ph.D. degree in 2008 from the Yerevan State University. Since 2009 he is a researcher at the Department of Information Engineering, University of Brescia, Italy. His research activity deals with the synthesis of semiconductor nanomaterials and nanocomposites as well as the investigations of their fundamental and functional properties for the applications in gas- and biosensors, energy and biomedicine. V. Galstyan has also worked on the development of technological methods required for realization of the flexible functional devices. In 2017 V. Galstyan obtained National Scientific Habilitation as an associate professor in Italian universities.
